title: Road to Invasion
summary: It's late 2021. Russia has gathered a huge force on three sides of Ukraine and says it's only training. Western intelligence says it's the build-up to an invasion. Read the evidence, decide what Russia is really planning — then watch what actually happens, from the first cruise missiles to the convoy that stalled outside Kyiv.

payload.directorBriefing: Russia has moved a lot of soldiers and equipment close to Ukraine. We need to work out whether this is sabre-rattling — or the real thing. Pin the evidence to the wall and look for patterns.

backgroundPrimer.label: The setup
backgroundPrimer.text: Russia and Ukraine have been on bad terms since 2014, when Russia took Crimea and started a low-level war in eastern Ukraine.

backgroundPrimer.label: What's new
backgroundPrimer.text: From summer 2021, satellite photos show Russia moving huge numbers of troops and tanks into bases close to Ukraine's borders.

backgroundPrimer.label: The question
backgroundPrimer.text: Is this a bluff to scare NATO into making concessions, or are they actually about to attack?

startingItems.title: Hundreds of tanks parked at Yelnya
startingItems.oneLineHint: Far more tanks than a normal exercise — 250km from the border

startingItems.title: Putin: 'Ukraine isn't a real country'
startingItems.oneLineHint: Long article saying Ukraine has no right to exist separately from Russia

startingItems.title: Field hospitals being built near the border
startingItems.oneLineHint: The size and number suggest preparing for real combat injuries

items.title: Hundreds of tanks parked at Yelnya
items.description: Satellite photos from Maxar Technologies show roughly 800 Russian tanks and armoured vehicles parked at the Yelnya training base, around 250km north of the Ukrainian border. That is far more equipment than a normal training exercise needs.
items.whyItMatters: Armies don't park this much kit unless they're getting ready to actually use it.

items.title: Putin: 'Ukraine isn't a real country'
items.description: President Putin publishes a 5,000-word article on the Kremlin website arguing that Russians and Ukrainians are 'one people' and that Ukraine has no historical right to exist as a separate country.
items.whyItMatters: Leaders write essays like this to set up the public reasoning for what comes next, not to talk themselves out of it.

items.title: Second pile of tanks at Pogonovo
items.description: In November 2021, satellite photos show a second large concentration of tanks and vehicles at the Pogonovo training area in Russia's Voronezh region. Combined with Yelnya, that is two big bases stacked with equipment pointed at Ukraine.
items.whyItMatters: Two big build-ups in different places suggests Russia is preparing to attack from more than one direction at once.

items.title: Field hospitals being built near the border
items.description: Open-source analysts spot newly built field-hospital sites near the Russian–Ukrainian border. Their size and number suggest they're meant for real combat casualties, not training injuries.
items.whyItMatters: You only build this many medical facilities when you expect a lot of wounded — i.e. real fighting.

items.title: Russia's list of demands to NATO
items.description: On 17 December 2021, Russia's Foreign Ministry publishes a draft 'Treaty on Security Guarantees', demanding NATO pulls back forces from Eastern Europe and promises never to admit Ukraine.
items.whyItMatters: The demands are written so NATO almost has to refuse — which then gives Russia a public 'reason' for whatever it does next.

items.title: US warns: invasion possible by Jan–Feb
items.description: The US Director of National Intelligence and other senior US officials publicly share declassified intelligence in November 2021. They assess that Russia is preparing a multi-front invasion of Ukraine as early as January or February 2022.
items.whyItMatters: Governments rarely declassify intelligence — they did it here to take away Russia's element of surprise.
items.imageCredit: US Director of National Intelligence, Nov 2021

items.title: Russia–Belarus joint exercise in February
items.description: Russia and Belarus announce a joint military exercise called 'Allied Resolve 2022' on Belarusian soil, scheduled for February 2022. The number of Russian troops involved is far larger than in previous years.
items.whyItMatters: An 'exercise' is the perfect cover story for moving large forces into position right next door to Ukraine.

items.title: Trains full of tanks heading west
items.description: Open-source researchers compile dozens of TikTok videos and rail-spotter posts showing Russian tanks and supporting vehicles being moved by train towards staging areas near Ukraine.
items.whyItMatters: Single-direction rail flow over weeks looks like an army stockpiling for a fight, not rotating kit for an exercise.
items.imageCredit: Konrad Muzyka, Rochan Consulting / OSINT collective

hotspots.label: Yelnya
hotspots.tooltip: Russian base ~250km north of Kyiv. Major tank build-up in 2021.

hotspots.label: Pogonovo
hotspots.tooltip: Russian base in the Voronezh region. Second tank build-up spotted in November 2021.

hotspots.label: Belarus border
hotspots.tooltip: Belarus is allied with Russia and borders northern Ukraine — only ~100km from Kyiv.

hotspots.label: Crimea
hotspots.tooltip: Russia took Crimea from Ukraine in 2014. Home base for Russia's Black Sea fleet.

hotspots.label: Kyiv
hotspots.tooltip: Ukraine's capital. Whoever holds Kyiv controls the country's leadership.

hotspots.label: Kharkiv
hotspots.tooltip: Ukraine's second-biggest city, in the east, near the Russian border.

hotspots.label: Donbas
hotspots.tooltip: Eastern region where Russia has backed separatists since 2014.

hotspots.label: Kherson
hotspots.tooltip: City in southern Ukraine. Controls the water supply that Crimea depends on.

payload.prompt: If Russia attacks, where do they go in? Click two places to draw a route. You have 3 routes — star the one you think is the main attack on the capital.

actors.name: Vladimir Putin
suggestedQuestions: Why is Russia making these demands of NATO right now?
Are you planning to attack Ukraine?
What outcome would make Russia pull its forces back?

actors.name: Sergey Lavrov
suggestedQuestions: What deal would Russia actually accept?
Why publish the security demands now, in public?
Are talks with the US going anywhere?

actors.name: Joseph R. Biden
suggestedQuestions: How sure are you that Russia will invade?
What will the US actually do if it happens?
Why have you declassified this intelligence in public?

actors.name: Jens Stoltenberg
suggestedQuestions: Will NATO defend Ukraine if it's attacked?
Are NATO members really on the same page?
What is NATO doing right now to deter Russia?

actors.name: Volodymyr Zelensky
suggestedQuestions: How likely do you think an attack actually is?
What does Ukraine need from the West right now?
Are Ukrainian forces ready if Russia invades?

actors.name: Emmanuel Macron
suggestedQuestions: What does Putin actually want, in your view?
Is there still a diplomatic way out?
What is Europe willing to offer Russia?

actors.name: Olaf Scholz
suggestedQuestions: Will Germany shut down Nord Stream 2 if Russia invades?
Can Europe replace Russian gas if it has to?
Where is Germany willing to draw the line?

actors.name: Xi Jinping
suggestedQuestions: What did China and Russia agree at the recent summit?
Will China back Russia if it invades Ukraine?
Is China worried about a major war breaking out in Europe?

payload.prompt: Late December 2021. Russia has put forces around Ukraine, demanded NATO pull back, and refuses to give ground. What do you think Russia will actually do?

signalsRecap.takeaway: Two huge tank build-ups, plus a third on Crimea, plus field hospitals — far bigger than any normal exercise.
Three possible attack lines: Belarus → Kyiv, Yelnya → Kharkiv, and Crimea → southern Ukraine.
An essay denying Ukraine's existence, plus demands on NATO that NATO can't accept — both look like setting up a public excuse.
The US has gone unusually public with its intelligence — a clear attempt to deter Russia by removing surprise.

options.text: Just bluffing — Russia will pull back if NATO offers some concessions.
options.hint: The demands could be a deliberate over-ask to force a smaller compromise. But this option ignores the size of the build-up and the field hospitals.

options.text: A small attack — Russia will only push into the Donbas region in eastern Ukraine.
options.hint: Russia already has forces in the Donbas backing separatists. A push there would be the cheapest, least risky option — but it doesn't explain why so much kit is gathered near Belarus and Crimea too.

options.text: A full invasion from multiple directions, including aiming for Kyiv.
options.hint: Three big build-ups (Yelnya, Belarus, Crimea) point at three different parts of Ukraine. The medical and logistics signs suggest something much bigger than a Donbas-only push.

options.text: Recognises the breakaway eastern regions, sends in 'peacekeepers', then escalates into a full invasion.
options.hint: This gives Russia a legal-sounding excuse for sending troops, then lets them keep going. It uses the political signals AND the military build-up — the most consistent with all the evidence so far.

newItems.title: Putin recognises Donetsk and Luhansk as 'independent'
newItems.description: President Putin gives an hour-long speech on Russian state TV recognising the two breakaway eastern Ukrainian regions as independent countries. This becomes the legal-sounding excuse for sending Russian troops in.
newItems.whyItMatters: It's the first half of the hybrid play — make it look legal first, then send the troops.

newItems.title: Putin announces 'special military operation'
newItems.description: In a pre-dawn televised speech on 24 February, President Putin announces what he calls a 'special military operation' to 'demilitarise' Ukraine. It is the public start of a full invasion.
newItems.whyItMatters: The wording avoids the word 'war' — but the scale of what follows shows the real plan.

newItems.title: Cruise missiles hit airfields and command sites
newItems.description: Around 4am Kyiv time, cruise missiles and air strikes hit Ukrainian airfields, command centres, and air-defence radar sites across the country.
newItems.whyItMatters: This is the standard opening move of a modern invasion — knock out the defender's air force before sending in ground troops.

newItems.title: Russian paratroopers attack Hostomel airfield
newItems.description: Russian paratroopers fly in by helicopter to seize Hostomel airfield just outside Kyiv. The plan is to use it as an air bridge — landing more troops directly on the doorstep of the capital.
newItems.whyItMatters: If Russia holds Hostomel, they can fly waves of soldiers straight into Kyiv. Whoever wins this fight shapes the early war.

connectionResolutions.explanation: Field hospitals plus a huge tank build-up = preparing for real combat, not training. This was the strongest single sign.
Both were the political setup for invasion — not alternatives to it. Russia wrote the public 'why' before doing the 'what'.
The US warning matched up exactly with the Belarus 'exercise' as cover for the northern push on Kyiv.
Trains kept feeding kit into Yelnya — a stockpile, not a normal exercise rotation.
Two big bases stacked with kit confirmed Russia was preparing to attack from more than one direction.
Western intelligence assessments were built directly on the satellite picture you saw at the start.
Logistics plus medical readiness together signalled real war, not exercises.

hotspots.label: Yelnya
hotspots.tooltip: Russian base ~250km north of Kyiv. Major tank build-up in 2021.

hotspots.label: Pogonovo
hotspots.tooltip: Russian base in the Voronezh region. Second tank build-up.

hotspots.label: Crimea
hotspots.tooltip: Russia took Crimea from Ukraine in 2014.

hotspots.label: Kharkiv
hotspots.tooltip: Ukraine's second city, near the Russian border.

hotspots.label: Donbas
hotspots.tooltip: Eastern region with Russian-backed separatists since 2014.

subDecision.prompt: Russia is striking first. What would you expect them to hit in the opening hour? (Pick all that apply.)

options.text: Airfields — so Ukrainian fighter jets can't take off.
Command centres — so Ukrainian leadership can't coordinate.
Air-defence radars — so Russian planes can fly freely.
Civilian areas — apartment blocks, schools, hospitals.

subDecision.prompt: Russian paratroopers have just landed at Hostomel airfield, right outside Kyiv. As Ukraine, do you send forces to take it back immediately?

options.text: Yes — if Russia keeps Hostomel, they can fly more troops straight into Kyiv.
No — focus on the Donbas, that's the bigger threat.

subDecision.prompt: A 40-mile-long Russian convoy is stalled north of Kyiv, not moving. Why has it stopped?

options.text: Ukrainian forces are ambushing it from the woods.
Logistics failure — running out of fuel, tyres, food.

annotatedReplayBeats.headline: What armies need behind the front line
annotatedReplayBeats.body: Field hospitals, blood supplies, and tanks parked in huge numbers don't show up for an exercise — they show up when an army is getting ready for real fighting. This was the strongest sign of what was coming.
annotatedReplayBeats.takeaway: Look at supplies, not just troops.

annotatedReplayBeats.headline: The main attack came from Belarus
annotatedReplayBeats.body: Most people guessed the main target would be the Donbas, in the east. The real main target was Kyiv, attacked from Belarus to the north. The Russia–Belarus 'joint exercise' was actually the cover story for moving the forces that would go for Kyiv.
annotatedReplayBeats.takeaway: Cover stories often tell you exactly what's about to happen.

annotatedReplayBeats.headline: The two-step move
annotatedReplayBeats.body: On 21 February, Russia 'recognised' the breakaway eastern regions, giving itself a legal-sounding excuse to send troops. Three days later, on 24 February, it launched a full invasion. The combination of fake diplomacy plus full invasion was the actual plan — not one or the other.
annotatedReplayBeats.takeaway: When you can't pick between two options, the real answer often uses ALL the signals.

annotatedReplayBeats.headline: Hostomel airfield decided a lot
annotatedReplayBeats.body: Russian paratroopers tried to seize Hostomel airfield outside Kyiv to fly more troops straight into the capital. Ukrainian forces denied it to them. With Hostomel lost, plus a 40-mile convoy stuck in mud and ambushes, Russia's plan to take Kyiv quickly fell apart.
annotatedReplayBeats.takeaway: One denied target can change the whole war.

teaserNextChapter.title: Battle of Kyiv: Russia's plan falls apart
teaserNextChapter.blurb: March 2022. Russian forces are stuck north of Kyiv. Ukrainian troops hunt Russian tanks down country roads. Will Kyiv hold?
